result:
It is a strange thing, to be awake in the small hours when the city has switched off most of its streetlights and the sky forgets to hide the stars. I sit at the kitchen table, the one that wobbles no matter how many folded napkins you wedge under its leg, and I listen to the refrigerator humming like a bored monk reciting half-remembered scripture. Everything is colder at three in the morning: the linoleum under my bare feet, the glass of water I keep refilling just to have something to do with my hands, the blue glow of the numbers on the stove that insist time is still moving forward even though the day feels fully spent. I am thinking, in the diffuse way one thinks while technically not thinking, about how a house stores the residue of every life that has passed through it the way old radios keep the faintest echo of every song they ever played. When I placed my palm against the wall yesterday, I swear the drywall gave back a pulse that did not belong to me.

When I was seven, my grandmother told me that dust is mostly flakes of human skin, and I have never quite forgiven her for the image. Now, two decades later, I walk through this rented bungalow and wonder whose epidermis glints in the slant of afternoon light. I inhale strangers by the lungful; they settle on my tongue with the taste of rusted keys and burnt toast. Perhaps that is why we develop allergies: the body staging a tiny mutiny against the trespass of foreign nostalgias. I sneeze and, somewhere in the rafters, a ghost says gesundheit.

Every possession I own is a negotiation with inertia. The books reproduce when I’m not looking; the mugs multiply like gentle porcelain fruit flies. I keep a cardboard box labeled “ cables & longing ” that I have moved through four states without opening. I no longer remember what converters and obsolete chargers wait inside, coiled like sleeping serpents, but I know the box is important the same way I know the moon is still there even when I haven’t bothered to check. Objects grow roots if you leave them in one place too long; they drink the daylight and whisper to one another in frequencies only dogs and insomnia can hear. Sometimes I pick up an item—a keychain from a college I never attended, a single garden glove stiff with decade-old soil—and feel the unmistakable vertigo of borrowed memory. Who lost you? Who loved you? Why did both of you stop?

I read once that the universe is flat like a sheet of paper, and I have been trying ever since to crease it so I can mail myself somewhere else. The mathematics of folding are merciless; every time you reduce the distance between two points you create a new edge that can cut. I picture astronomers in starched white coats sliding metallic rulers across that cosmic parchment, shaking their heads at my irresponsible dog-ears. Still, I practice origami with tram tickets and grocery receipts, hoping to perfect the technique on a smaller scale. If I can just get the angle right, I’ll step from Tuesday to last May, from the laundromat to my mother’s kitchen the final time she made perfect rice and the air smelled of cardamom instead of morphine. I’ll arrive with a quiet pop, like a soap bubble kissing the floor, and fold the moment into my pocket before anyone notices I was gone.

There are 847 types of silence, and I have catalogued them the way birder’s log warblers. The silence that follows a slammed door is charcoal grey, brittle at the edges. The silence of a hospital at 3 a.m. is a milky jelly that fills your ear canals until you hear your own blood arranging deck chairs. The silence just before the first drop of rain on parched earth is the color of held breath. Tonight’s silence is velvet, heavy and dark purple, the kind that used to be reserved for coronations or the出轨的丈夫 coming home to find the note on the pillow. It drapes itself over the back of my chair and purrs. I do not pet it; I know better.

I keep losing words. Not in the dramatic stroke-induced way, but casually, the way one misplaces a pen. Yesterday I forgot the name of the plastic tip at the end of a shoelace and had to gesture like a charades player approaching an orgasm: “ the thing, the tiny crown, the aglet! ” My grandfather would have called this “ the beginning of the end, ” but he also believed that spitting on a screwdriver made it work better, so his authority is negotiable. Still, the absences form a hidden constellation across my conversations: galaxies of circumlocution where entire civilizations of vocabulary drift dark and unlit. Sometimes I fantasize about staging a rescue mission, a sort of linguistic Coast Guard that braves the neural fog to haul the drowning syllables aboard a bright orange helicopter. We would airlift them home, wrap them in thermal blankets, offer hot sweet tea until they remembered how to mean.

The neighbor’s cat has decided I am a disappointment. She appears at the window precisely when I begin to feel optimistic, presses her paw against the glass, and stares with the cold appraisal of a loan officer reviewing an application for a third mortgage. I suspect she is employed by some celestial auditing agency, dispatched to ensure I remain marginally ashamed of my progress. When I tried to explain that I am, in fact, doing my best, she yawned so wide I saw thetiny pink cavern at the back of her throat, a private cathedral where echoed the faint sound of my unfinished novels. I closed the curtains; she sat on the sill for another hour, tail twitching in Morse code: Try harder, try harder.

At dawn the world exhales a sigh of relief so subtle only the sparrows hear it. They react by quarreling louder, tiny feathered politicians filibustering the morning. I pour coffee into the mug that says “World’s Okayest Son” and watch the light change its mind about the color of the garden fence. By increments, the night’s velvet silence is traded for the thin aluminum of commuter traffic, the sky rehearsing its daily magic trick of turning from bruise to champagne. I think about how every sunrise is an unsolicited reset button, how we are all participants in an experiment no one bothered to control. Somewhere a physicist is calculating the exact second when the expansion of the universe will exceed the speed of light and all future horizons will go dark; somewhere else a child is discovering the reflective power of a puddle and will spend the entire afternoon trying to bounce the sun back into her mother’s eyes. Both efforts are equally earnest, equally doomed, equally necessary.

I decide to forgive the refrigerator for its monotonous sermon. I decide to forgive myself for not yet mailing that box of cables & longing to wherever such parcels are meant to arrive. I open the window; the cat has vanished, leaving only a pawprint of condensation like a ghost signature on an unpublished contract. The air smells of bread beginning its rise in the bakery two blocks away, and I remember that yeast is a fungus which, left to its own devices, will eat and burp and party until it dies of alcohol poisoning, leaving behind a delicious corpse we call civilization. Maybe that is the most honest form of legacy: to ferment so enthusiastically that your final exhale becomes someone else’s breakfast. I breathe in the future toast and feel almost ready to be useful, or at least edible, to whatever version of the day is currently proofing on the windowsill of the world.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
